content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
October 14, 2016 
Rhoann Ponseti 
Member, Public Art Advisory Committee 
Town of Windsor 
764 McClelland Drive 
Windsor, CA 95492 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-205 
Dear Ms. Ponseti: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political 
Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering advice. 
(/11 re Oglesby ( 1975) I FPPC Ops. 71.) 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section I 090. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit you from taking part in the governmental decision on which of three 
alternative design options to recommend to the Town Council for the renovation of the water 
fountain located in the Windsor Town Green community park (the "fountain renovation design 
decision") given that you own your residence located approximately 350 feet from the fountain? 
CONCLUSION 
No. The Act does not prohibit you from taking part in the fountain renovation design 
decision because that decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on 
your real-property interest in your residence. 
1 The Political Refonn Act is con1ained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are lo Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS 
File No. A-16-205 
Page No. 2 
The Town of Windsor recently entered into an agreement with an architectural finn to 
provide design services for the renovation of the Town's water fountain located in the Windsor 
Town Green, a 4.5-acre community park located in the Town's Old Downtown. The fountain runs 
the length of McClelland Drive and is currently run by mechanical equipment housed underground. 
Although the renovation will include a new structural and artistic design for the fountain, the 
foremost priority for the renovation is to provide Town maintenance staff with a safe, accessible, 
aboveground way to regularly service the fountain's mechanical equipment. 
The Town's Public Art Advisory Committee ("PAAC'') will soon consider, and receive 
public input regarding, three alternative design options for the renovation of the fountain provided 
by the firm, and will recommend one of those options to the Town Council. The PAAC is 
composed of one Town Councilmember, one Town Park and Recreation Commissioner, and five 
citizens appointed at large by the Town Council for four-year tenns. The Town considers the PAAC 
to be a decision-making body and treats PAAC members as public officials. 
You are a citizen member of the P AAC, appointed at large by the Town Council. You own 
your residence, a condominium within a 13-unit condominium complex, within approximately 350 
feet from the center of the Windsor Town Green, where the fountain is located. In our phone 
conversation on October 5, 2016, you advised that you also own a residential rental property within 
the Town located beyond 500 feet from the fountain. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87 l 00 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a decision, 
within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the official's interests. Section 87103 also identifies the financial 
interests that may give rise to a conflict of interest under the Act. Of those interests, the facts 
presented and the decision at issue implicate your real property interest in your residence. (See 
Section 87103(b).) 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
For a financial interest not "explicitly involved"1 in the decision at issue, the financial effect 
of the decision on an official's interest is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic 
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 1870l(b).) 
S An official's financial interest is explicitly involved in a governmental decision if the interest is a named 
party in, or subject of, the decision. A financial interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial, or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the 
financial interest, or if the decision affects a real property interest described in Regulation I 8702.2(a)(l )-(6). 
(Regulation 18701 (a),) 


File No. A-16-205 
Page No. 3 
Regulation 18702.2(a)( 11) sets forth the materiality standard applicable to a decision's 
effect on an official's real property interest if the decision would affect real property value located 
within 500 feet of the property line of the official's real property: If the decision would have a 
reasonably foreseeable measurable impact on the official's real property interest, the financial effect 
is material. 
Although the fountain renovation design decision may lead to an improvement to the 
aesthetics of the neighborhood surrounding your residence, there is no indication from the facts 
presented that the mere renovation of the existing fountain would result in a reasonably foreseeable 
measurable impact on your real property interest in your residence. Therefore, we find that the 
decision would not have a reasonably foreseeable material effect on your real property interest in 
your residence. 
Accordingly, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit you from taking part in the fountain 
renovation design decision.3 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division 
3 You also own residential rental property located beyond 500 feet from the water fountain, and you have 
vanous financial interesLc; related to that property under the Act However. we have determined that the fountain 
renovation design decision would not have a measureable impact your residence, and your residential rental property is 
further removed from the fountain than your residence. Furthermore, we have previously determined that a PAAC 
member who owns a commercial office building in close pro:<imity to the fountain, and who had similar interests 
implicated by the decision as those at issue here with respect to your residential rental property, was not prohibited from 
taking part in the fountain renovation design decision. (Mendelsohn Advice Letter, No. A-16-154.) Consistent with our 
determination in Menclelso/rn, we find that the fountain renovation design decision would not have a reasonably 
foreseeable material financial effect on your interests related to your residential rental property.
